footer.title: Computational Algebra Problem Solver
footer.tagline: © {{year}} · Turning matrices into obedience since v1.0

home.title: Computational Algebra Problem Solver
home.subtitle: Choose one of the problems below to explore linear algebra, number theory, coding theory, and eigenvalues. Clean interface, clear explanations, accurate computations.

problem1.title: Triangular matrices
problem1.description: Verify whether the inverse of a triangular matrix is also triangular. Verify whether the product of two triangular matrices is also triangular.

problem2.title: Decomposition into symmetric and skew-symmetric matrices
problem2.description: Show that any matrix can be written uniquely as the sum of a symmetric matrix and a skew-symmetric matrix.

problem3.title: Hermitian matrix and its inverse
problem3.description: Check whether a matrix is Hermitian. If it is, check whether its inverse is also Hermitian.

problem4.title: Solve a 4×4 linear system using Gauss
problem4.description: Solve a linear system with 4 equations and 4 unknowns using Gaussian method.

problem5.title: Rank and inverse using Gauss method
problem5.description: Compute the rank of a matrix using Gaussian elimination and, if possible, compute its inverse using the Gauss–Jordan method.

problem6.title: Order and idempotent element in (ℤₙ, ·)
problem6.description: Given (ℤₙ, ·), compute the order of an element and check whether it is idempotent modulo n.

problem7.title: Matrix order in (M₂(ℝ), ·) and idempotency
problem7.description: Compute the order of a 2×2 matrix under multiplication (if it exists) and check whether it is idempotent.

problem8.title: Code parameters (n, M, d, s, t, ρ)
problem8.description: For a code in (Fq)^n, compute the main parameters: n, M, d, s, t, and the covering radius ρ.

problem9.title: Check if a code is linear in (Fq)^n
problem9.description: Check whether a given code is linear (closed under addition and scalar multiplication modulo q).

problem10.title: List all codewords from a generator matrix
problem10.description: Display all codewords of a code given its generator matrix G (modulo q).

problem11.title: Codeword validation using parity-check matrix
problem11.description: Read a parity-check matrix and a set of words. Display the ones that are codewords (zero syndrome).

problem12.title: Characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues (2×2, 3×3)
problem12.description: Compute the characteristic polynomial and the eigenvalues of a 2×2 or 3×3 matrix, displaying both the polynomial and its roots.

problem13.title: Galois group for a degree 3 or 4 polynomial
problem13.description: Determine the Galois group of a degree 3 or 4 polynomial using the discriminant and the resolvent.

problem14.title: Euclidean algorithm, linear combination, and continued fraction
problem14.description: Using Euclid’s algorithm, compute gcd(d) for two natural numbers (a,b), write a linear combination of (a,b,d), and display the continued fraction of a/b.

errors.unexpectedComputation: An unexpected error occurred during computation.

problem1.title: Problem 1 – Triangular matrices
problem1.subtitle: Check whether the inverse of a triangular matrix is also triangular. Check whether the product of two triangular matrices is also triangular. Enter two square matrices A and B below, or use the presets.
problem1.presetsHint: Quickly choose a pair of matrices to see how the inverse and product behave for triangular matrices.
problem1.sizeHint: Matrices A and B are square and have the same size.
problem1.explain1: A matrix is upper triangular if all entries below the main diagonal are zero, and lower triangular if all entries above the main diagonal are zero.
problem1.explain2: The inverse of an invertible triangular matrix is triangular of the same type. The product of two triangular matrices of the same type (upper or lower) is also triangular of the same type.
problem1.summaryB: Matrix B and the product A·B
problem1.doesNotExistNotInvertible: does not exist (the matrix is not invertible).
problem1.productType: Type of the product C = A · B

errors.sizeRange: Matrix size must be an integer between 1 and 6.

upper3x3.label: Two 3×3 upper triangular matrices
upper3x3.description: Example where the inverse and product remain upper triangular.

lower3x3.label: Two 3×3 lower triangular matrices
lower3x3.description: The product and inverse (if it exists) remain lower triangular.

nonTriangular.label: A non-triangular matrix
nonTriangular.description: Example where matrix A is not triangular.

problem2.presetsHint: Quickly choose one of the matrices below to see how the decomposition works. You can then edit the values manually.

problem2.explainSkewSimmetric: The skew-symmetric component K is computed as:

errors.presetNotSquare: The preset matrix is not square.

simple2x2Matrix.label: Simple 2×2 matrix
simple2x2Matrix.description: Small example, easy to verify by hand.

symmetric3x3Matrix.label: Symmetric 3×3 matrix

problem3.title: Problem 3 – Hermitian matrix and its inverse

subtitle.before: Check whether a matrix is Hermitian. For real entries, a matrix is Hermitian if it is symmetric:
subtitle.after: If it is Hermitian and invertible, check whether its inverse is also Hermitian.

problem3.presetsHint: Quickly choose a matrix to test (invertible Hermitian / singular Hermitian / non-Hermitian).

p1.before: Definition: a matrix is Hermitian if
p1.middle: (equal to its conjugate transpose). For real-valued matrices, this becomes simply

status.hermitianYes: The matrix is Hermitian (for real entries).
status.hermitianOnlySquare: Hermitianity is checked only for square matrices.

inverse.exists: The matrix is invertible. The inverse is shown below:
inverse.singular: The matrix is Hermitian, but it is not invertible (singular).
inverse.onlyIf: The inverse and this check are computed only if the matrix is square and Hermitian.

errors.colRange: Number of columns must be an integer between 1 and 8.

realInversableHermitianMatrix.label: Invertible Hermitian (real) matrix
realInversableHermitianMatrix.description: Real symmetric example with an inverse.

realSingularHermitianMatrix.label: Singular Hermitian (real) matrix
realSingularHermitianMatrix.description: Symmetric, but determinant zero → no inverse.

notHermitianMatrix.label: Not Hermitian
notHermitianMatrix.description: Example that is not symmetric (for real inputs).

subtitle.before: Solve a linear system of 4 equations with 4 unknowns using Gaussian elimination (on the augmented matrix
subtitle.after: Depending on the ranks, the system can have a unique solution, infinitely many solutions, or no solution.

problem5.title: Problem 5 – Rank and inverse using Gauss method

subtitle.before: Compute the rank of a matrix using Gaussian elimination and, if the matrix is square and invertible, compute its inverse using the Gauss–Jordan method on the augmented matrix

problem5.presetsHint: Quickly choose a matrix to test the rank, the echelon form, or the inverse.

p1.after: into an echelon form using elementary row operations. The rank is the number of non-zero rows in the echelon form.

p2.before: To compute the inverse of a square matrix, we build the augmented matrix
p2.middle: and apply Gauss–Jordan until we obtain

explain.p3: If the matrix is not square or its determinant is zero, the inverse does not exist and the matrix is singular.

summary.rank: The rank of matrix A is:

inverse.exists: The matrix is invertible. Below is the inverse

problem6.title: Problem 6 – Order and idempotent element in (ℤₙ, ·)

subtitle.before: Given the multiplicative group (ℤₙ, ·) of residues modulo
subtitle.middle1: , compute the order of an element
subtitle.middle2: and check whether it is idempotent

problem6.inputTitle: Enter the data: modulus n and element a
problem6.modulusHint: n ≥ 2. Use not-too-large values to clearly see the steps.
problem6.elementHint: a can be any integer; computations are done modulo n.

p1.afterA: in (ℤₙ, ·) is the smallest positive integer

p3.before: To compute the order, we multiply:

info.normalized: The normalized value of a modulo n:

unit.isUnit: The element a is a unit in (ℤₙ, ·), so its order is well-defined.
unit.notUnit: The element a is not a unit in (ℤₙ, ·), so its order in the group is not defined.

order.notFound: No order was found within the computed step limit (n may be too large).

notUnit.after: is not a unit, it has no order in (ℤₙ, ·).

no.after: is not idempotent: a² ≢ a (mod n).

problem6.stepsTitle: Computed steps for powers of a modulo n
problem6.stepsNote: Look for the first exponent k where the value becomes 1 — that is the order of a, if it exists.

errors.modulusMin: The modulus n must be an integer at least 2.

problem7.title: Problem 7 – Order of a matrix in (M₂(ℝ), ·) and idempotent
problem7.subtitle: Given a matrix A ∈ M₂(ℝ). Determine the order of A under multiplication (the smallest k ≥ 1 with A^k = I, if it exists) and check whether it is idempotent (A² = A).
problem7.presetsHint: Examples with finite order (I, −I, rotation) and examples without finite order (scaling, projection).
problem7.explainP1: The order of a matrix (in the group sense) is the smallest k ≥ 1 such that A^k = I. For many real matrices, such a k does not exist (infinite order).
problem7.explainP2: A matrix is idempotent if A² = A.
problem7.notFoundIntro: If the order is not found within the chosen limit, two typical cases are:
problem7.notFoundB1: the matrix has infinite order (no k with A^k = I)
problem7.notFoundB2: the order exists, but it is larger than the selected limit
problem7.foundWithinLimit: For this example, a k with A^k = I was found within the selected limit.

preview.title: First powers (preview)
preview.note: The preview shows up to the first 10 powers. For large powers, values may grow quickly and numerical errors may appear.

errors.maxPowerRange: Max power must be an integer between 1 and 500.
errors.tolerancePositive: Tolerance must be a positive number (e.g. 1e-10).

identity.label: Identity (order 1)
identity.description: I has order 1 and is idempotent.

projection-idempotent.label: Projection (idempotent)
projection-idempotent.description: P^2 = P, but it generally does not have finite order.

scale-2.label: Scaling (no finite order)
scale-2.description: 2I does not reach I for n>0.

problem8.title: Problem 8 – Code parameters (n, M, d, s, t, ρ)
problem8.subtitle: Given a code C ⊆ (Zq)^n. We display the parameters: n (length), M (number of words), d (minimum distance), t (error-correcting capability), ρ (covering radius), and s (redundancy, for linear codes).
problem8.presetsHint: Quickly choose a small code to test.

explain.p1: n = the length of a word, M = the number of distinct words.
explain.p2: d = the minimum Hamming distance between two distinct codewords. t = ⌊(d−1)/2⌋.
explain.p3: ρ = the maximum, over all vectors, of the minimum distance to the code (covering radius). For large q^n, computing ρ is too expensive and is omitted.

linearity.title: Linearity and parameter s
linearity.notePower: For a linear code, typically M = q^k.
linearity.linearButNoK: The code appears linear, but M is not an exact power of q, so k and s cannot be reliably inferred from these data.
linearity.nonLinearS: The parameter s (redundancy n−k) is standardly defined for linear codes.

uniqueWords.title: Distinct codewords
uniqueWords.note: The list is normalized modulo q and contains no duplicates.

errors.modulusRange: Modulus must be an integer between 2 and 13.
errors.wordCountRange: The number of words must be an integer between 1 and 64.
errors.wordLengthRange: Word length must be an integer between 1 and 12.

z2-nonlinear.label: Nonlinear code (Z₂), n=3
z2-nonlinear.description: A set of words that does not form a linear code.

problem9.title: Problem 9 – Check if a code is linear
problem9.subtitle: A code C ⊆ (Zₘ)ⁿ is linear if it contains the zero word and is closed under addition and scalar multiplication (mod m).
problem9.presetsHint: Quickly pick a set of words to test.

inputNote.before: Note: entries are automatically reduced modulo

conditions.textBefore: 0 ∈ C, a+b ∈ C for all a,b ∈ C, and α·a ∈ C for any scalar α.

counterexample.title: Counterexample (if any)
counterexample.sumNotInCode: The sum is not in the code.
counterexample.productNotInCode: The product is not in the code.
counterexample.missingZero: The code does not contain the zero word, so it cannot be linear.
counterexample.noneFound: No simple counterexample was found (or the code is linear).

errors.wordCountRange: Number of words must be an integer between 1 and 32.
errors.wordLengthRange: Word length must be an integer between 1 and 12.
errors.inconsistentLengths: All words must have the same length.
errors.unexpectedCheck: An unexpected error occurred during the check.

not-linear-missing-zero.label: Not linear (missing 0)
not-linear-missing-zero.description: Does not contain the zero word.

not-linear-not-closed.label: Not linear (not closed under +)
not-linear-not-closed.description: Contains 0, but is not closed under addition.

problem11.title: Problem 11 – Validate codewords using a parity-check matrix

subtitle.middle1: of a code and a set of words of length
subtitle.after: ). Words with zero syndrome are codewords.

problem11.presetsHint: Quickly choose a parity-check matrix and a set of words to demonstrate the feature. You can then edit values manually.
problem11.inputTitle: Enter the parity-check matrix H and the words

inputs.columnsLabel: Number of columns of H (word length)

explain.p1: For each word v, we compute the syndrome s = H · vᵀ (mod q). If s = 0, then v is a codeword.
explain.p2: The parity-check matrix H encodes all linear equations that a valid codeword must satisfy.
explain.p3: A nonzero syndrome indicates errors or that the vector is not in the code.

summary.noneValid: None of the entered words is a codeword.

errors.modulusRange: Modulus q must be an integer between 2 and 10.
errors.rowsRange: Number of parity-check rows must be between 1 and 8.
errors.colsRange: Number of columns must be an integer between 2 and 12.
errors.wordCountRange: Number of words must be an integer between 1 and 12.
errors.unexpected: An unexpected error occurred during computation.

binary-hamming-7-4.label: Binary Hamming (7,4) code
binary-hamming-7-4.description: Classic parity-check matrix for the Hamming (7,4) code.

problem12.title: Problem 12 – Characteristic polynomial and eigenvalues (2×2, 3×3)

subtitle.middle1: of size 2 or 3, compute the characteristic polynomial
subtitle.middle2: and the eigenvalues (the roots of this polynomial). Below you can choose the matrix size and enter the coefficients.

problem12.presetsHint: Quickly choose a matrix to demonstrate the feature. You can edit the values afterwards.
problem12.inputTitle: Enter the matrix and choose the size

inputs.sizeHint: This problem only covers matrices of size 2 or 3.

explain.p1: The characteristic polynomial of a matrix A is p(λ) = det(λI − A). The eigenvalues are the roots of this polynomial.
explain.p2: For 2×2 matrices, the characteristic polynomial has the form: p(λ) = λ² − tr(A) λ + det(A).
explain.p3: For 3×3 matrices, the characteristic polynomial is cubic, and real eigenvalues are obtained numerically as real roots of this cubic.

eigen.title: Eigenvalues (real)
eigen.found: The matrix has the following real eigenvalues (approximated):
eigen.none: No real eigenvalues (real roots) were found for the characteristic polynomial.

errors.presetInvalidSize: The preset has an invalid size.

general-2x2.label: General 2×2
general-2x2.description: Example with two distinct real eigenvalues.

problem13.title: Problem 13 – Galois group of a polynomial (degree 3 or 4)
problem13.subtitle: We determine the Galois group (over ℚ) for a degree 3 or 4 polynomial using classical criteria: rational roots, discriminant, and (for degree 4) the resolvent cubic.
problem13.presetsHint: Quickly choose a polynomial to test.

coeffsHint.before: Enter the coefficients in descending order:

summary.resolventIntro: Resolvent cubic (integer coefficients after clearing denominators):

explanations.leadingCoefficientZero: Leading coefficient is 0, so the polynomial is not of the requested degree.

cubic.hasRationalRootReducible: The polynomial has a rational root, so it is reducible over Q.
cubic.splitsCompletelyTrivial: It splits completely into linear factors over Q, so the splitting field is Q and the Galois group is trivial.
cubic.linearTimesIrreducibleQuadraticC2: Typical case: linear factor times an irreducible quadratic, so the splitting field adds one quadratic root and the Galois group is C2.
cubic.noRationalRootIrreducible: No rational root, so the cubic is irreducible over Q (rational root theorem).
cubic.discriminantSquareA3: The discriminant is a square in Z, so the Galois group is A3 ≅ C3.
cubic.discriminantNotSquareS3: The discriminant is not a square, so the Galois group is S3.

quartic.hasRationalRootReducible: The quartic has a rational root, so it is reducible over Q.
quartic.reducibleDependsOnFactorization: For reducible quartics, the Galois group depends on the exact factorization (linear/quadratic factors). This implementation focuses on irreducible quartics for S4/A4/D4/V4/C4 classification.
quartic.noRationalRootProceedIrreducibleCase: No rational root found. We proceed with the standard irreducible-quartic classification approach (discriminant + resolvent cubic).
quartic.resolventRationalRootCount: Resolvent cubic has {{count}} rational root(s) (counting distinct roots found by the rational-root test).
quartic.resolventIrreducibleDiscSquareA4: Resolvent cubic irreducible and discriminant square, so the Galois group is A4.
quartic.resolventIrreducibleDiscNotSquareS4: Resolvent cubic irreducible and discriminant not a square, so the Galois group is S4.
quartic.resolventHasRootDiscNotSquareD4: Resolvent has a rational root and discriminant is not a square, so the Galois group is D4.
quartic.discSquareResolventSplitsV4: Discriminant square and resolvent splits (3 rational roots), so the Galois group is V4 (Klein).
quartic.discSquareResolventHasRootNotSplitC4: Discriminant square and resolvent has a rational root but does not split completely, so the typical outcome is C4.

notes.p1: Note: for degree 3, the (A3/S3) classification is standard for irreducible polynomials over ℚ.
notes.p2: For degree 4, we use the discriminant and the resolvent cubic (a common course-level decision rule). If the polynomial is reducible, the full classification depends on the factorization type.

errors.unexpected: An unexpected error occurred during computation.

cubic_s3.label: Cubic: x³ − 2
cubic_s3.description: Irreducible cubic, discriminant not a square → S3.

cubic_a3.label: Cubic: x³ − 3x − 1
cubic_a3.description: Often has a square discriminant → A3 (C3) in many examples.

quartic_dihedral.label: Quartic: x⁴ − 5x² + 6
quartic_dihedral.description: Has special structure; try classification via the resolvent.
